title: 08-3-3 Xiao Huang - Thermal Barrier Coating and their Impacts
text: xhuang@mae.carleton.ca Functions of Thermal Barrier Coatings and Their Impact on Gas Turbine Component Life Xiao Huang, P. Eng., Ph.D. Associate Professor Dept. of Mechanical & Aerospace Engineering Carleton University © Xiao Huang, Oct. 2008 xhuang@mae.carleton.ca Outline  Functions and development of thermal barrier coating systems  Compositions and microstructures of thermal barrier coatings  Failure mechanisms  Life of thermal barrier coating system  Limitations and future directions  Discussions xhuang@mae.carleton.ca Thermal Barrier Coating (TBC) in GT Subramania et al, TurboExpo 2008 GT2008-51532 Clarke and Levi, Annu. Rev. Mater. Res. 33(2003)383–417 S i e m e n s S • A coating system providing heat shielding, corrosion, oxidation and erosion resistance • 4 layer complex, dynamic system Siemens SGT6-5000F Siemens SGT6-4000F xhuang@mae.carleton.ca Failure Mechanisms of GT Components  Overheating (burning(√)  Fatigue (√)  Creep (>0.4Tm) (√)  Oxidation (√)  Hot Corrosion from salt deposit (√)  Erosion (√)  Base metal microstructure deterioration (aging, rafting, TCP phase formation) (√)  Wear TurboExpo 2006, GT2006-90137 TurboExpo 2006, GT2006-90075 Nagy, et al, Liburdi Turbine Services X. Huang, AERO 3700, Course Notes, Carleton University xhuang@mae.carleton.ca Historical Development in GT Materials Clarke and Levi, Annu. Rev. Mater. Res. 33(2003)383–417 H. Xu et al, Thin Solid Films, 334(1998)98-102. 50ºC reduction on metal surface = 50 yr superalloy development xhuang@mae.carleton.ca Historical Development in TBC Gen I -1970s Plasmas sprayed (PS) MCrAlY and 7YSZ on combustion can & afterburner Black fracture surface Thick porous laminar TBC (~1 mm) Up to 500 K reduction when combined

title: 08-3-3 Xiao Huang - Thermal Barrier Coating and their Impacts
text: al, Thin Solid Films, 334(1998)98-102. 50ºC reduction on metal surface = 50 yr superalloy development xhuang@mae.carleton.ca Historical Development in TBC Gen I -1970s Plasmas sprayed (PS) MCrAlY and 7YSZ on combustion can & afterburner Black fracture surface Thick porous laminar TBC (~1 mm) Up to 500 K reduction when combined with cooling Gen II - 1980s Low-pressure plasma- sprayed (LPPS) MCrAlY and PS 7YSZ on high pressure turbine nozzles and transition ducts White failure mode (cracking in TBC) Gen III - Late1980s LPPS NiCoCrAlY or Pt modified aluminide and EB-PVD columnar 7YSZ on rotational components Typically 250 µm thick Failure in TGO or interfaces Maricocchi et al., J. of Thermal Spray Tech. vol. 6(2)(1997)193-198 (GE Aviation) Bose et al., J. of Thermal Spray Tech. vol. 6(2)(1997) 99-104 (Pratt & Whitney) xhuang@mae.carleton.ca Functions of Top and Bond Coats  7YSZ top coat (TC) • Increase creep life of substrate by reducing steady state metal surface temperature • Reduce thermal stresses during transient stage (heat up and cool down) • Environmental protection • Increase bond coat creep life (reduced rumpling of bond coat at >DBTT) • Reduce cooling air requirement • Enable high TIT (efficiency and emission)  MCrAlY or aluminide bond coat (BC) • Oxidation and corrosion resistance • Bonding of top coat to substrate  Coating life determines inspection interval ~150°C Subramania et al, Turbo Expo 2008, GT2008-51532 Ashby et al, Materials-engineering, science, processing and design, 1st ed. Elsevier, 2007 20ºC=100% increase in life xhuang@mae.carleton.ca Requirements of Top Coating Conduction

title: 08-3-3 Xiao Huang - Thermal Barrier Coating and their Impacts
text: coat (BC) • Oxidation and corrosion resistance • Bonding of top coat to substrate  Coating life determines inspection interval ~150°C Subramania et al, Turbo Expo 2008, GT2008-51532 Ashby et al, Materials-engineering, science, processing and design, 1st ed. Elsevier, 2007 20ºC=100% increase in life xhuang@mae.carleton.ca Requirements of Top Coating Conduction / Radiation Ceramic Coating Bond Coat Metallic Substrate Hot Gas TH Cooling Gas TL TGO Temperature Profile without TBC Temperature Profile with TBC 100~150oC Requirement for TBCs  Thermal insulation  High melting points  Microstructure stability  Fracture toughness  Matching thermal expansion coefficient  Low elastic modulus 7wt% Y2O3 partially stabilized ZrO2 (7YSZ) (porous or vertically cracked structure to accommodate strains) Clarke et al, Annu. Rev. Mater. Res. 33(2003)383–417 D. M. Wang et al. Canadian Aeronautics and Space Journal, Vol.50 (2)(2004)107-114. xhuang@mae.carleton.ca Deficiencies of 7YSZ  Fast cooling from APS or EB-PVD →t’  Transparent to thermal radiation  Increased thermal conductivity and elastic modulus upon thermal exposure (sintering) at 1200ºC  Changing thermal stresses  Decomposition of t’ phase at 1200ºC leading to cracking t’ →t + c →m + c  High oxygen diffusivity - oxidation of bond coat Chevalier, Annu. Rev. Mater. Res. 37(2007)1-32 Siegel et al, Materials Science and Engineering, A245(1998)150-159. D. M. Wang et al, Canadian Aeronautics and Space Journal, Vol.50 (2) (2004)107-114. ~60ºC YSZ xhuang@mae.carleton.ca Failure Mechanisms for TBC Top Coat  TBC system prone to failure under thermal cycling  TBC failure – immediate component failure from overheating for modern engine

title: 08-3-3 Xiao Huang - Thermal Barrier Coating and their Impacts
text: et al, Materials Science and Engineering, A245(1998)150-159. D. M. Wang et al, Canadian Aeronautics and Space Journal, Vol.50 (2) (2004)107-114. ~60ºC YSZ xhuang@mae.carleton.ca Failure Mechanisms for TBC Top Coat  TBC system prone to failure under thermal cycling  TBC failure – immediate component failure from overheating for modern engine  Typical failure modes: • FOD or erosion • Top coating cracking (excessive temperature – phase transformation and sintering related cracking) • CMAS damage • Hot corrosion of top coat (leaching out yttria and phase transformation) • Top coat spalling (TGO formation and cracking at the interface- further discussed in BC failure)  Coating process, operating temperature, time and cycle dependent  Mostly experiment based life predication approach Subramania et al, Turbo Expo 2008, GT2008-51532 S. Kramer et al, J. Am. Ceram. Soc., 91(2)(2008), 576-583 Clarke et al, Annu. Rev. Mater. Res, 33(2003)383-417 At higher operating temperature, top coat layer-by-layer de-lamination due to sintering effect Coating spalling, white part=TBC and grey part=bond coat Coating spalling, white part=TBC and grey part=bond coat xhuang@mae.carleton.ca New TBC Top Coat  Requirements • Lower thermal conductivity • Stable structure • Improved fracture toughness  Modification to existing 7YSZ  New structures (layers)  New coating compositions  Stable at higher temperature  Promising directions • EB-PVD multiple layered structure • Multiple dopants • Rare earth zirconate Maloney MJ. 2000. U.S. Patent No. 6,117,560 Vassen et al. J. Am. Ceram. Soc. 83(2000) 2023–28 X. Huang, J. Eng. Gas Turbines and Power, 2009, Vol. 131 J.

title: 08-3-3 Xiao Huang - Thermal Barrier Coating and their Impacts
text: coating compositions  Stable at higher temperature  Promising directions • EB-PVD multiple layered structure • Multiple dopants • Rare earth zirconate Maloney MJ. 2000. U.S. Patent No. 6,117,560 Vassen et al. J. Am. Ceram. Soc. 83(2000) 2023–28 X. Huang, J. Eng. Gas Turbines and Power, 2009, Vol. 131 J. singh et al, Penn state University Configuration B Configuration C xhuang@mae.carleton.ca Bond Coat  As bond coat or stand alone  Aluminide (β-NiAl, (Ni,Pt)Al, PtAl2+(Ni,Pt)Al) • High DBTT (600-900ºC) • CVD, pack cementation, slurry • Pt plating+ VPA • DVD NiAlPt alloy • Diffusion HT  MCrAlY overlay • β-Ni(Fe,Co)Al + γ • Lower DBTT (200-700ºC) • 2-4 X higher cost • Coated component has service life 2-5 times of bare component • NiCrAlY-oxidation resistant • CoCrAlY-hot corrosion resistant  Compositions • Al: high temperature oxidation resistance • Cr: hot corrosion to sulphur containing salts • Y: oxide film adhesion • Pt: slow diffusion and improve oxidation resistance • Si: type II hot corrosion resistant Nicholls et al. Surf. Coat. Technol. 149 (2002) 236–244 Z. Yu et al. Surf Coat. Technol. 201 (2006) 2326–2334 xhuang@mae.carleton.ca Oxide Formers Stable – large negative free energy of formation Dense – no appreciable volume changes Slow growth rate The science and engineering of materials, D. R. Askeland and P.P. Phule, 5th ed. Thomson xhuang@mae.carleton.ca Detrimental Effect of TBC to Substrates  Fatigue life reduction • Crack initiates in the coating during cyclic loading • Propagation to substrate  Microstructure degradation by internal stresses (coating

title: 08-3-3 Xiao Huang - Thermal Barrier Coating and their Impacts
text: changes Slow growth rate The science and engineering of materials, D. R. Askeland and P.P. Phule, 5th ed. Thomson xhuang@mae.carleton.ca Detrimental Effect of TBC to Substrates  Fatigue life reduction • Crack initiates in the coating during cyclic loading • Propagation to substrate  Microstructure degradation by internal stresses (coating process and operation) • γ rafting (anisotropic coalesence) • SX recrystallization  Secondary reaction zone with single crystal superalloy Suresh et al, Fatigue cracking in materials with brittle surface coatings 29(1993) 237-242 Itoh et al, Fatigue Fract Eng Mater Struct, 24(2001) 843-854 xhuang@mae.carleton.ca Bond Coat Related Failures  As standalone corrosion/oxidation resistant coating • Al forms Al2O3 (thermally grown oxide-TGO) during application, post coating heat treatment and during service • Al consumption due to TGO and inter-diffusion • When used alone TGO spalls and rebuilt until reaching critical Al content • Hot corrosion (molten salt)  As bond coat • TGO thickens over time (Top coat provide oxygen sources due to defects such as pores, cracks and oxygen vacancies) • Mixed oxide formation • Internal strain buildup Cracking Spallation Failure of coating system Turbo Expo 2006, GT2006-90748 Gurrappa, Surface and Coatings Technology 139 (2001) 272-283 Sohn et al. Surface and Coatings Technology 146 –147 (2001) 70–78 xhuang@mae.carleton.ca Life Predication Approaches  Existing Models: • TGO and critical crack size • Failure maps • COSIM (critical Al concentration) • Heckel (critical Al concentration) • DICTRA software – inter-diffusion microstructure map  Materials, processes and service specific  Semi-empirical  Mostly based

title: 08-3-3 Xiao Huang - Thermal Barrier Coating and their Impacts
text: Coatings Technology 146 –147 (2001) 70–78 xhuang@mae.carleton.ca Life Predication Approaches  Existing Models: • TGO and critical crack size • Failure maps • COSIM (critical Al concentration) • Heckel (critical Al concentration) • DICTRA software – inter-diffusion microstructure map  Materials, processes and service specific  Semi-empirical  Mostly based on TGO thickness and cumulative mechanical strain  Calibrated by experimental data  Lack of knowledge in damage mechanism, physical and mechanical properties of the material system as function of temperature and strain rate  Lack of oxidation and diffusion models for multiple component system  Material property changes must to incorporated into the lifing models Goswami et al. High Temperature Materials and Processes, Vol.23(3) (2004)211-236 I.T. Spitsber et al. Materials Science and Engineering A, 394(2005) 176-191 A.G. Evans et al., Prog. Mater. Sci., 46 (2001) 505-553 R.S. Heckel et al. Thin Solid Film, 45(1977) 367 J.A. Nesbitt et al. Metall. Trans., 18A (1987) 2075 K.V. Dahl et al. Defect and Diffusion Forum Vols. 258-260 (2006) 73-78 xhuang@mae.carleton.ca Examples of Life Assessment  For stand alone MCrAlY or aluminides – chemical failure • Aluminum consumption(8 at% Al=coating life) • % of beta phase (0% β=limit) • Diffusion layer thickness (model GT)  For TBC system – mechanical failure • Stress based (CTE mismatch, TGO growth stress, external…) • Strain based: cyclic loading, out-of-phase compressive strain determines the total fatigue life of TBC  Empirical or experimental based method P. Krukovsky, Surface and Coatings Technology 177 –178 (2004) 32-36 P. Kennard

title: 08-3-3 Xiao Huang - Thermal Barrier Coating and their Impacts
text: GT)  For TBC system – mechanical failure • Stress based (CTE mismatch, TGO growth stress, external…) • Strain based: cyclic loading, out-of-phase compressive strain determines the total fatigue life of TBC  Empirical or experimental based method P. Krukovsky, Surface and Coatings Technology 177 –178 (2004) 32-36 P. Kennard Wright, Materials Science and Engineering, A245(1998) 191-200 Turbo Expo 2006, GT2006-90138 Weijie Chen, et al, ITSC, 2009 strain mechanical total ) ( = ∆ ∆ = tot m n tot m f k N ε ε critical ernal external thermal σ σ σ σ > + + int nt RT Q A d ) exp(− = xhuang@mae.carleton.ca Challenges  Material system - TBC less than ideal • TC: oxygen and radiation transparent, microstructure instability and limited thermal conductivity for EB-PVD coating • BC: higher CTE, brittle and high DBTT, use of precious metals (Pt, Rd…), composition variations  Process • Lack of standard microstructure (process and vendor sensitive) • Complexity and variation of coating application techniques  Life predication • Semi-empirical in nature • Each case must be validated by engine experience and field feedback • No universal model xhuang@mae.carleton.ca Points for Discussion  Development of new materials and processes?  Reliable life predication method?  In-situ monitoring?  Self-healing coating system? Goswami et al. High Temperature Materials and Processes, 23(3) (2004)211-236 xhuang@mae.carleton.ca Acknowledgement  Researchers at NRC: Drs. P. Patnaik, P. Au, R.
